paper title: Bringing the genetically minimal cell to life on a computer in 4D
summary: We present a whole-cell spatial and kinetic model for the ∼100 min cell cycle of the genetically minimal bacterium JCVI-syn3A.
method summary: Mechanistic modeling and simulation, calibrated against experiments where available.
contribution: Establishes or extends the minimal-cell experimental and conceptual foundation used by later whole-cell models.
limitation: Model behavior depends on assumptions, parameter availability, and the scope of experimental calibration.
future work: Expand the model with richer spatial detail, broader validation, and tighter integration with new measurements.

paper title: A Whole-Cell Computational Model Predicts Phenotype from Genotype
summary: Introduces the first genome-scale whole-cell model of Mycoplasma genitalium by coupling many mechanistic submodels to predict phenotype from genotype.
method summary: Integrated 28 cellular process modules with literature-derived parameters and simulation-based phenotype prediction.
contribution: Established the canonical modern whole-cell-modeling framework and made genotype-to-phenotype simulation a concrete systems-biology target.
limitation: Built on sparse and heterogeneous data for one small bacterium, so many parameters and assumptions were only weakly constrained.
future work: Generalize the framework to richer organisms, improve parameter inference, and add more direct experimental calibration.

paper title: Fundamental behaviors emerge from simulations of a living minimal cell
summary: Extends minimal-cell whole-cell simulation to show that cell-scale behaviors can emerge from integrated subcellular models in JCVI-syn3A.
method summary: Combined a living minimal-cell simulation with experimental benchmarking to test emergent behaviors across growth and gene-expression processes.
contribution: Demonstrated that integrated minimal-cell models can recover nontrivial emergent behaviors rather than only isolated subsystem outputs.
limitation: Still limited by coarse-grained spatial assumptions and incomplete measurements for several molecular processes.
future work: Push the model toward fuller cell-cycle coverage, better spatial realism, and stronger experimental falsification.

paper title: Metabolism, cell growth and the bacterial cell cycle
summary: Reviews how bacterial metabolism, biomass accumulation, and cell-cycle progression are mechanistically coupled rather than independent layers.
method summary: Conceptual review synthesizing physiological, metabolic, and cell-cycle literature across bacteria.
contribution: Provides the physiological logic needed to link metabolic state, growth rate, and replication timing in whole-cell models.
limitation: A review cannot resolve organism-specific parameter values or directly validate a predictive cell-scale simulator.
future work: Translate the conceptual links into quantitative models with organism-specific measurements and perturbation tests.

paper title: Integrating cellular and molecular structures and dynamics into whole-cell models
summary: Reviews how structural biology, imaging, and dynamics data can be fused to move whole-cell models from well-mixed abstractions toward physical cells.
method summary: Field review covering integrative modeling workflows that connect molecular structures, localization data, and cell-scale simulations.
contribution: Clarifies how structural and spatial data can become first-class inputs for next-generation whole-cell models.
limitation: Primarily a roadmap; it does not itself deliver a new validated whole-cell model.
future work: Standardize multiscale data integration and build pipelines that update whole-cell models as structural datasets grow.

paper title: How to build the virtual cell with artificial intelligence: Priorities and opportunities
summary: Cells are essential to understanding health and disease, yet traditional models fall short of modeling and simulating their function and behavior.
method summary: Narrative synthesis of prior literature to organize the field and compare modeling strategies.
contribution: Advances whole-cell-model scope, validation, or organization at the system level.
limitation: Review-oriented framing; it does not itself resolve missing parameters or validate a new mechanistic model.
future work: Use the review framework to prioritize model coupling, better data standards, and cross-scale validation.

paper title: Simultaneous cross-evaluation of heterogeneous E. coli datasets via mechanistic simulation
summary: The extensive heterogeneity of biological data poses challenges to analysis and interpretation.
method summary: Mechanistic modeling and simulation, calibrated against experiments where available.
contribution: Constrains gene-expression modules by quantifying transcription, translation, or ribosome assembly behavior.
limitation: Model behavior depends on assumptions, parameter availability, and the scope of experimental calibration.
future work: Expand the model with richer spatial detail, broader validation, and tighter integration with new measurements.

paper title: Cross-evaluation of E. coli’s operon structures via a whole-cell model suggests alternative cellular benefits for low- versus high-expressing operons
summary: Uses an E. coli whole-cell model to test how different operon architectures alter cellular tradeoffs across low- and high-expression regimes.
method summary: In silico operon rewiring experiments performed inside a mechanistic whole-cell simulation of E. coli.
contribution: Shows how whole-cell models can evaluate genome organization hypotheses that would be expensive to test exhaustively in vivo.
limitation: Findings inherit the assumptions and calibration limits of the underlying E. coli whole-cell model.
future work: Pair simulation-guided operon redesign with experimental genome engineering to validate predicted benefits.

paper title: Toward a Whole-Cell Model of Ribosome Biogenesis: Kinetic Modeling of SSU Assembly
summary: Builds a kinetic model of small-subunit ribosome assembly as a tractable subsystem on the path toward whole-cell ribosome biogenesis modeling.
method summary: Constructed a kinetic assembly model constrained by biogenesis intermediates and experimental timing information.
contribution: Breaks a major macromolecular process into a form that can be plugged into larger whole-cell simulations.
limitation: Covers one subsystem and therefore cannot explain cell-wide coupling without additional modules.
future work: Integrate SSU assembly with transcription, large-subunit assembly, growth, and resource allocation models.

future work: Map the discovered factors into predictive mechanistic models of constriction timing, morphology, and partitioning.

paper title: Entropy as the driver of chromosome segregation
summary: Argues that polymer entropy can itself help drive segregation of replicated bacterial chromosomes, offering a physical complement to active transport models.
method summary: Conceptual and theoretical analysis of chromosome segregation using polymer-physics arguments.
contribution: Introduced an influential physical principle that later chromosome-organization models can encode or test.
limitation: Entropy alone is unlikely to explain all organism-specific segregation behaviors without active protein factors.
future work: Combine entropic and protein-mediated mechanisms in quantitative simulations tied to live-cell measurements.
